title: Грамматика — Grammar A to Z
description: A comprehensive A-to-Z reference of Russian grammar for advanced English-speaking learners, with full paradigm tables for nouns, adjectives, pronouns, numerals, and verbs, plus transliterated examples and concise usage notes.

units.title: 1. Nouns: Gender & Declension Classes
units.summary: How to determine grammatical gender from the dictionary (nominative) ending and how nouns sort into three declension classes.

blocks.html: Every Russian noun has one of three genders — masculine, feminine, or neuter — which you can usually predict from its nominative singular ending. Gender controls the agreement of adjectives, pronouns, and (in the past tense) verbs. Gender is a grammatical property of the word, not necessarily of its referent, though for animate beings the two usually coincide.

blocks.caption: Predicting gender from the nominative-singular ending
rows: But natural-male nouns are masc.: папа, дядя, мужчина.
день (m., day), словарь (m., dictionary); ночь (f.), дверь (f.)
Must be memorized; abstract/-ость nouns are feminine.
A small closed class of ~10 nouns with special declension.

blocks.html: The soft-sign trap. A final -ь tells you nothing about gender by itself: день (day) is masculine but ночь (night) is feminine. Two useful heuristics: nouns in -тель and the months are masculine; abstract nouns in -ость / -есть (e.g. радость joy, новость news) and nouns ending in a hushing consonant + ь (ночь, дочь, мышь) are feminine.
The three declension classes group nouns by how they take case endings. They cut across gender as shown below.

blocks.caption: The three declension classes
rows: masculine in consonant/-й and all neuter

blocks.html: Numbering conventions differ between textbooks (some call the masculine/neuter group "1st"). The membership is what matters; the labels above follow the common Russian school tradition (школьная грамматика).

items.en: This is a new student. (masc., consonant ending)
Here is an interesting book. (fem., -а ending)
Open the big window. (neut., -о ending)
What a long night! (fem., 3rd declension)

units.summary_ar: كيفية تحديد الجنس النحوي من نهاية الكلمة في المعجم (حالة الرفع)، وكيف تنقسم الأسماء إلى ثلاث فئات إعرابية.

units.title: 2. The Case System Overview
units.summary: What each of the six cases does, in one line, before the detailed paradigms.

blocks.html: Russian marks a noun's role in the sentence with case — a change in ending — rather than relying mainly on word order. There are six cases. Mastering their functions first makes the ending tables that follow far easier to absorb.

blocks.caption: The six cases at a glance
rows: The direct object of a transitive verb.
Location and topic; only used after prepositions.

blocks.html: The prepositional is the only case that never appears without a preposition (в, на, о, при, по). Vestiges of older cases survive: a locative-2 in stressed -у́ (в лесу́ 'in the forest', на берегу́ 'on the shore') and a partitive genitive in -у (чашка ча́ю 'a cup of tea').

items.en: The student (nom.) reads a book (acc.) in the library (prep.).
I gave the book (acc.) to a friend (dat.).
He writes with a pen (instr.).
This is my brother's (gen.) house.

units.summary_ar: ما الذي تؤديه كل حالة من الحالات الإعرابية الست في سطر واحد، قبل عرض الجداول التفصيلية للصيغ.

units.title: 3. Nominative & Accusative (with Animacy)
units.summary: The subject case and the direct-object case, and the crucial animacy rule that makes animate accusatives look like genitives.

blocks.html: The nominative is the citation form and marks the grammatical subject — the doer of the action. The accusative marks the direct object: the thing directly affected by a transitive verb (читать книгу, to read a book).

The animacy rule is one of the central facts of Russian morphology. For masculine singular nouns and for all plural nouns, the accusative depends on whether the referent is alive: animate accusative = genitive; inanimate accusative = nominative.

Feminine singular nouns in -а/-я have their own distinct accusative (-у/-ю) and so are unaffected by animacy: Я вижу студентку (animate) and Я вижу книгу (inanimate) take the same -у ending. Animacy only ever matters where acc. would otherwise be ambiguous with nom.

items.en: The brother (nom.) loves his sister (acc. fem.).
I know this professor. (animate masc. acc. = gen.)
I bought a new computer. (inanimate masc. acc. = nom.)
We invited friends. (animate plural acc. = gen.)

units.summary_ar: حالة الفاعل وحالة المفعول به المباشر، وقاعدة الحياة المهمة التي تجعل المفعول به الحيّ يشبه حالة الإضافة (المضاف إليه).

units.title: 4. Genitive Case
units.summary: The 'of' case — possession, absence, quantities, and the count rules after numbers.

blocks.html: The genitive (родительный падеж) is the workhorse of Russian. Its core meaning is the English of: it expresses possession and relationship between two nouns, but it also signals absence, partitive quantity, and is governed by many prepositions and numbers.

blocks.caption: Genitive endings: singular
rows: -ы (spelling: -и after г к х ж ч ш щ)

blocks.caption: Genitive endings: plural (the hardest paradigm in Russian)
rows: masc. after ж ч ш щ

blocks.html: The zero ending of feminine/neuter plurals often forces a fill (fleeting) vowel to break up a final consonant cluster: окно → окон, сестра → сестёр, девушка → девушек, письмо → писем. Learn these case by case.

items: Possession / relation ('of'): книга студента — the student's book; центр города — the centre of the city.
Absence with нет / не было / не будет: нет времени — there is no time; у меня нет машины — I have no car.
After most quantity words: много денег (much money), мало людей (few people), стакан воды (a glass of water).
Partitive: купить хлеба — to buy some bread (vs. весь хлеб, the whole bread).
Governed by prepositions: из, от, до, без, для, у, около, после, против, вокруг, кроме.

blocks.html: The number rules are essential and exam-favourite. The case of the counted noun depends on the final digit of the number:

blocks.caption: Case of the noun after cardinal numbers
rows: два студе́нта, три кни́ги, четы́ре окна́
пять студе́нтов, де́сять книг, оди́ннадцать окон

items.en: I don't have a brother. (gen. of absence)
This is my parents' house. (gen. pl. possession)
He bought five tickets. (5+ → gen. pl.)
I drank a cup of tea. (partitive gen.)
There are three students in the class. (2-4 → gen. sg.)

units.summary_ar: حالة الإضافة (المضاف إليه) — الملكية، والغياب، والكميات، وقواعد العدّ بعد الأعداد.

units.title: 5. Dative Case
units.summary: The recipient case — indirect objects, age, liking (нравиться), and the prepositions к and по.

blocks.html: The dative (дательный падеж) answers кому? / чему? (to whom? / to what?). Its core role is the indirect object — the recipient or beneficiary — but it is also the case of the experiencer in many impersonal constructions, including age and liking.

Feminine -а/-я and -ь nouns take dative -е and -и respectively, which are identical to their prepositional singular forms — a helpful overlap. (Exception: nouns in -ия/-ие take dative -ии: лекция → лекции.)

items: Indirect object: Я дал другу книгу — I gave my friend a book.
Age (with dative + год/года/лет): Мне двадцать лет — I am 20 (lit. 'to me [there are] 20 years').
нравиться (to please / to like): Мне нравится Москва — I like Moscow (lit. 'Moscow is pleasing to me'). The liker is dative; the liked thing is the subject.
Impersonal predicates: Мне холодно (I am cold), ему трудно (it's hard for him), нам нужно (we need to), можно/нельзя + dative.
Preposition к (toward / to a person): Я иду к врачу — I'm going to the doctor.
Preposition по (along / according to / via): идти по улице (along the street), по телефону (by phone), специалист по физике (a specialist in physics).

items.en: I like this music.
How old are you? (lit. how many years to you?)
The professor explained the rule to the students.
items.ku: پڕۆفیسۆرەکە ڕێسایەکەی بۆ خوێندکاران ڕوون کردەوە.

items.en: We are going to grandmother's. (к + dat.)
He was bored. (dative experiencer)

units.summary_ar: حالة المفعول غير المباشر (المتلقّي) — المفعول غير المباشر، والعمر، والإعجاب (نَرافِتسا)، وحرفا الجر «ك» و«پو».

units.title: 6. Instrumental Case
units.summary: The 'by means of' case — instruments, accompaniment with с, predicate nouns with быть, and a set of spatial prepositions.

blocks.html: The instrumental (творительный падеж) answers кем? / чем?. Its core meaning is the means or instrument of an action (writing with a pen), but it has a wide range of further uses, including accompaniment, predicate nouns, and spatial relations.
Feminine -ой/-ей has a stylistic variant -ою/-ею common in poetry and set phrases. After hushing consonants (ж ч ш щ ц) the unstressed ending is -ем and stressed is -ом/-ой: товарищем, отцом, душой.

items: Means / instrument (no preposition): писать ручкой (with a pen), есть ложкой (with a spoon).
с = 'together with': чай с лимоном (tea with lemon), я говорил с другом (I spoke with a friend). Distinguish from instrumental without с (= means).
Predicate noun after быть / стать / работать / являться: Он был врачом (he was a doctor), она стала учителем (she became a teacher), я работаю инженером (I work as an engineer).

Spatial prepositions с/со, над, под, перед, за, между: над столом (above the table), под кроватью (under the bed), перед домом (in front of the house), между нами (between us), за углом (around the corner).
Time of day / season: утром (in the morning), зимой (in winter), вечером (in the evening) — frozen instrumentals.

blocks.html: Be careful: заниматься, интересоваться, гордиться, пользоваться and other verbs govern the bare instrumental: Я интересуюсь историей — I am interested in history.

items.en: I write with a pencil. (means)
We drink coffee with milk. (accompaniment)
He wants to become a doctor. (predicate)
The cat is sleeping under the table. (под + instr.)
I do sports. (verb-governed instr.)

units.summary_ar: حالة الأداة («بواسطة») — الأدوات، والمصاحبة مع «س»، والأسماء الخبرية مع الفعل «بيت»، ومجموعة من حروف الجر المكانية.

units.title: 7. Prepositional / Locative Case
units.summary: The case that only follows prepositions — location (в/на) and topic (о about).

blocks.html: The prepositional (предложный падеж) is unique: it never occurs without a governing preposition. Its two main jobs are location (after в and на) and topic (after о/об, 'about').

Locative-2 (second prepositional). A small group of masculine nouns take stressed -у́ after в/на to mean physical location: в лесу́ (in the forest), на берегу́ (on the shore), в саду́ (in the garden), в углу́ (in the corner), на полу́ (on the floor), в году́ (in the year). The regular -е survives in non-locative meanings (о ле́се — about the forest).

items: в + prep. = inside / in (enclosed): в городе (in the city), в университете (at the university), в России (in Russia).
на + prep. = on / at (surfaces, events, certain places): на столе (on the table), на работе (at work), на концерте (at the concert), на Кавказе, на Украине/в Украине.
о / об / обо + prep. = about (topic): думать о будущем (to think about the future), книга об истории (a book about history). Use об before a vowel, обо before мне/всём.
при + prep.: при университете (attached to the university), при Петре (under [the reign of] Peter).

items.en: The book is lying on the table.
We live in a big city.
They are talking about politics.
The children are playing in the garden. (locative-2)
I dream about the sea.

units.summary_ar: الحالة التي لا تأتي إلا بعد حرف جر — المكان (في/على) والموضوع (عن «о»).

units.title: 8. Full Noun Declension Tables
units.summary: Consolidated singular and plural paradigms for all three declensions, all six cases.

blocks.html: Here are the complete reference paradigms. Use these to cross-check anything in the case chapters above. Representative nouns: стол (m. inanim.), студент (m. anim.), музей (m. soft), окно/море (n.), книга/неделя (f. 1st), ночь (f. 3rd).

Spelling rules that override the tables. (1) After г к х ж ч ш щ write и not ы. (2) After ж ч ш щ ц write unstressed е not о (товари́щем, but отцо́м when stressed). (3) Never write я ю after ж ч ш щ. These cause many of the surface variations above.

Irregular plurals to memorize: друг → друзья (gen. друзей), брат → братья, сын → сыновья, дерево → деревья; город → города́, дом → дома́, учитель → учителя́ (stressed -а́ plurals); человек → люди, ребёнок → дети; мать → матери, дочь → дочери (insert -ер-).

items.en: Our friends have a new car. (irregular gen. pl.)
There are many houses in the cities.
I was talking with my mother. (instr. sg., insert -er-)

units.summary_ar: صِيَغ إعرابية مجمّعة للمفرد والجمع لجميع الفئات الإعرابية الثلاث وجميع الحالات الست.

units.title: 9. Adjectives: Agreement & Declension
units.summary: Long-form adjectives agree in gender, number, and case; hard vs soft stems; plus short-form adjectives.

blocks.html: Russian adjectives agree with their noun in gender, number, and case. The long (attributive) form is the dictionary form (e.g. новый, new). Stems are either hard (новый) or soft (синий, blue); a few are 'mixed' due to spelling rules (русский, большой, хороший).

* The masculine and plural accusative follow the animacy rule just like nouns: with animate nouns the adjective uses the genitive form (нового студента, новых студентов); with inanimate nouns it uses the nominative form (новый стол, новые столы).

The soft paradigm simply substitutes the soft-series vowels (я/ю/и/е) for the hard ones (а/у/ы/о). Mixed-stem adjectives like русский, большой, хороший follow hard endings but obey the spelling rules (и after к/г/х/ш; unstressed е after hushers): русское but большо́е, хоро́шее.

The short form of an adjective exists only in the nominative, agrees in gender/number, and functions as a predicate (after a copula), never as an attribute. It is formed from the stem with the endings -Ø (m.), -а (f.), -о (n.), -ы/-и (pl.).

* A fleeting vowel often appears in the masculine short form to break a cluster: умный → умён, интересный → интересен, больной → болен. Short forms tend to express a temporary or relative state, while long forms express a permanent/inherent quality: Она больна (she is ill right now) vs. Она больная (she is a sick/sickly person).

items.en: I am reading an interesting book. (fem. acc.)
We talked about the new students. (prep. pl.)
items.ku: ئێمە لەسەر خوێندکارە نوێیەکان قسەمان کرد.

items.en: This city is very beautiful. (short form, predicate)
I must go. (short form должен)

units.summary_ar: الصفات في صيغتها الطويلة تطابق الاسم في الجنس والعدد والحالة؛ الجذوع الصلبة مقابل اللينة؛ بالإضافة إلى الصفات في صيغتها القصيرة.

units.title: 10. Comparatives & Superlatives
units.summary: Forming and using the comparative and superlative degrees of adjectives and adverbs.

blocks.html: The comparative has two types. The simple/synthetic comparative is a single indeclinable word ending in -ее (or -ей) used predicatively: новее (newer), интереснее (more interesting). The compound/analytic comparative uses более + the plain adjective and can be attributive: более интересная книга (a more interesting book).

To say 'than', use either чем + nominative, or simply put the standard of comparison in the genitive (only possible with the simple comparative): Москва больше, чем Петербург = Москва больше Петербурга.

The superlative is most often formed with самый + adjective (declined, agreeing fully): самый интересный фильм (the most interesting film). A bookish synthetic superlative in -ейший / -айший exists (новейший — newest/latest, величайший — greatest), often with an elative ('extremely') rather than comparative sense. The phrase comparative + всех / всего means 'most of all': Он бегает быстрее всех — he runs fastest of all.

items.en: This problem is harder than the previous one. (gen. comparison)
items.ku: ئەم مەسەلەیە لە مەسەلەی پێشوو قورستترە.

items.ru: Он говорит по-русски лучше, чем я.
items.tr: On govorit po-russki luchshe, chem ya.
items.en: He speaks Russian better than I do. (чем + nom.)
items.ku: ئەو لە من باشتر بە ڕووسی قسە دەکات.

items.ru: Это самый красивый город в мире.
items.tr: Eto samyy krasivyy gorod v mire.
items.en: This is the most beautiful city in the world.
Most of all I love to read.
items.ku: لە هەموو شتێک زیاتر، خوێندنەوەم خۆشدەوێت.

units.summary_ar: تكوين واستعمال صيغتي التفضيل النسبي والمطلق للصفات والأحوال.

units.title: 11. Pronouns
units.summary: Personal, possessive, demonstrative, and interrogative pronouns with full case tables.

blocks.html: Pronouns decline like nouns/adjectives but with many idiosyncrasies. The personal pronouns are the most irregular and the most frequent — learn this table cold.

The н- rule: the 3rd-person forms (он/она/они) add an initial н- after a preposition: у него (he has), с ней (with her), к ним (toward them). Without a preposition they stay bare: его книга (his book), я вижу его (I see him).

blocks.caption: Possessive pronoun мой ('my') — full declension (твой, свой decline identically)

blocks.html: наш / ваш decline like мой but with stem наш-/ваш- (наш, наша, наше, наши; нашего, нашему…). его / её / их ('his / her / their') are indeclinable — they never change form. свой is the reflexive possessive ('one's own'), referring back to the subject: Он любит свою работу (he loves his [own] work) vs. Он любит его работу (someone else's).
Distinguish the neuter pronoun это meaning 'this is / it is' (invariable: Это книга, Это мой брат) from the agreeing demonstrative этот/эта/это/эти ('this [noun]').
чей agrees with the possessed noun: чей (m.), чья (f.), чьё (n.), чьи (pl.). какой declines exactly like a hard adjective (какой, какая, какое, какие).

items.en: I have a question. (lit. by me there is)
I am thinking about you.
She took her (own) book.
Whose car is this?
With whom were you talking?

units.summary_ar: الضمائر الشخصية والملكية والإشارية والاستفهامية مع جداول كاملة للحالات الإعرابية.

units.title: 12. Numerals
units.summary: Cardinal and ordinal numbers, their tricky case government, and agreement.

blocks.html: Russian numerals are notorious because the number governs the case of the counted noun, and the numerals themselves decline. First, the case government — the single most tested point.

blocks.caption: Case the counted noun takes (nominative context)

blocks.html: 2 has gender: два with masc./neut. (два стола, два окна) but две with fem. (две книги). Один agrees fully (один, одна, одно, одни). The teens 11–14 take the genitive PLURAL despite ending in 1–4 (одиннадцать книг). When the whole quantified phrase is itself in an oblique case, the number and noun simply agree in that case: с двумя друзьями (with two friends, instr.).

blocks.caption: Cardinal numbers 1–20, plus tens and large

blocks.html: Ordinal numbers ('first, second…') are adjectives: they decline and agree fully in gender, number, and case. In a compound ordinal, only the last word is ordinal: двадцать первый (twenty-first), в тысяча девятьсот сорок пятом году (in 1945).
* третий is a soft adjective with -ь- in its endings: третий, третья, третье, третьи; третьего, третьему…

items.ru: У меня две сестры и один брат.
items.tr: U menya dve sestry i odin brat.
items.en: I have two sisters and one brother.
items.ku: من دوو خوشک و یەک برام هەیە.

items.en: There are twenty-five students in the group. (gen. pl.)
items.ku: لە گرووپەکەدا بیست و پێنج خوێندکار هەن.

items.en: Today is the third of May. (ordinal + gen. of month)
He came with two friends. (number declined, instr.)

units.summary_ar: الأعداد الأصلية والترتيبية، وحُكمها الإعرابي المعقّد للأسماء، والمطابقة.

units.title: 13. Verb Basics: Aspect & Conjugation
units.summary: The aspect system (imperfective vs perfective), the two conjugation classes with present-tense tables, and the verb быть.

blocks.html: Aspect is the organizing principle of the Russian verb. Almost every verb belongs to an aspectual pair: an imperfective (несовершенный вид) and a perfective (совершенный вид) partner with the same lexical meaning but different views of the action.

blocks.caption: Imperfective vs perfective: the core contrast
rows: completed, whole action with a result
has ONLY past and future (no present!)

items: How pairs form: by prefix (делать → сделать, писать → написать), by suffix/stem change (решать → решить, давать → дать, покупать → купить), or suppletively (говорить → сказать, брать → взять).
When to use imperfective: describing a process ('I was reading'), habits ('I read every day'), general statements, and after phase verbs (начать, продолжать).
When to use perfective: a single completed action with a result ('I read the whole book'), a sequence of finished events, the future as a definite accomplished fact.

blocks.html: In the present tense (imperfective only), verbs belong to one of two conjugation classes, distinguished by the vowel in the endings: the 1st conjugation (-е-/-ё-, with -ут/-ют in the 3pl) and the 2nd conjugation (-и-, with -ат/-ят in the 3pl).

Stress can shift and consonants can mutate. In the 2nd conjugation a stem-final consonant often mutates only in the 1sg: любить → люблю (б→бл), видеть → вижу (д→ж), просить → прошу (с→ш). Some verbs are 'mixed' or irregular (хотеть, бежать).

The verb быть ('to be') has essentially no present-tense form — Russian uses zero copula: Я студент = 'I am a student'. The form есть survives mainly in existential 'there is' and possession (У меня есть…). Its past is был/была/было/были and its future is the irregular буду, будешь, будет, будем, будете, будут.

items.ru: Я читаю эту книгу уже неделю.
items.tr: Ya chitayu etu knigu uzhe nedelyu.
items.en: I've been reading this book for a week. (imperf., process)
items.ku: من ماوەی هەفتەیەکە ئەم کتێبە دەخوێنمەوە.

items.ru: Я прочитал эту книгу за один день.
items.tr: Ya prochital etu knigu za odin den'.
items.en: I read this book in one day. (perf., result)
items.ar: قرأتُ هذا الكتاب في يوم واحد.
items.ku: من ئەم کتێبەم لە یەک ڕۆژدا خوێندەوە.

items.en: He speaks Russian and English.
items.ku: ئەو بە ڕووسی و ئینگلیزی قسە دەکات.

items.en: There used to be a park here. (past of быть)

units.summary_ar: نظام الجهة (الفعل غير التام مقابل التام)، وفئتا التصريف مع جداول زمن المضارع، والفعل «بيت» (يكون).

units.title: 14. Past & Future Tenses
units.summary: Past tense agreement by gender/number, and the two ways of forming the future from each aspect.

blocks.html: The past tense is formed from the infinitive stem + the suffix -л, and — remarkably — it agrees with the subject in gender and number, not person. This is because it descends from an old participle.

With вы as a polite singular, use the plural -ли (Вы читали). A few verbs lose the -л in the masculine: нести → нёс (but несла, несло, несли), мочь → мог (могла), идти → шёл (шла, шло, шли — suppletive).
The future is built differently for each aspect — this is a direct payoff of the aspect system.

blocks.caption: The two futures
rows: Я буду читать (I will read / be reading)
the present-tense endings on the perfective stem

blocks.html: Crucial consequence: the perfective verb has no present tense. Its 'present-looking' conjugation (прочитаю, напишу, скажу) is in fact its future. So я пишу = 'I am writing' (imperf. present) but я напишу = 'I will write' (perf. future).

items.en: Yesterday she read all evening. (imperf. past, fem.)
items.ku: دوێنێ ئەو بە درێژایی ئێوارە خوێندیەوە.

items.en: Tomorrow I will be working. (compound future)

items.ru: Я напишу письмо и отправлю его.
items.tr: Ya napishu pis'mo i otpravlyu yego.
items.en: I will write the letter and send it. (perf. future, sequence)
They have already left. (perf. past, suppletive шли)

units.summary_ar: مطابقة زمن الماضي بحسب الجنس والعدد، والطريقتان لتكوين المستقبل من كل جهة فعلية.

units.title: 15. Imperative, Conditional, Reflexive & Verbs of Motion
units.summary: Commands, the бы conditional/subjunctive, -ся reflexive verbs, and the paired verbs of motion with prefixes.

blocks.html: The imperative (command) is formed from the present/future stem. Drop the 3pl ending; if the stem ends in a vowel add -й(те), otherwise add -и(те) (stressed) or -ь(те) (unstressed). Add -те for polite/plural.

Aspect matters in commands too: perfective for a specific one-off request (Закройте окно! — Close the window!), imperfective for general instructions, invitations, or (with не) prohibitions (Не закрывайте окно! — Don't close the window). The 1pl 'let's' uses Давай(те) + infinitive/future: Давайте пойдём.

The conditional / subjunctive uses the particle бы (often after a comma in clauses) together with the past-tense form of the verb, for any time reference. It expresses hypothetical, wished-for, or politely softened actions.

items.ru: Если бы у меня было время, я бы поехал в Москву.
items.tr: Yesli by u menya bylo vremya, ya by poyekhal v Moskvu.
items.en: If I had time, I would go to Moscow. (бы + past)
items.ar: لو كان لديّ وقت، لذهبتُ إلى موسكو.

items.en: I would like to ask. (polite softening)

blocks.html: Reflexive verbs carry the postfix -ся (after a consonant) / -сь (after a vowel). Beyond literal reflexive meaning ('oneself'), -ся marks the reciprocal, the passive, the intransitive/middle, and many lexicalized verbs.

blocks.caption: Functions of -ся, with умываться ('to wash oneself')
rows: The house is being built by workers.

blocks.html: Conjugation of -ся verbs: add -ся after a consonant ending and -сь after a vowel ending — учусь, учишься, учится, учимся, учитесь, учатся. Note 2sg учишься (after -ь, treated as consonant).

Verbs of motion form a special system. Unprefixed, each comes in a pair: a unidirectional/determinate verb (one specific trip, in progress) and a multidirectional/indeterminate verb (habitual, round-trip, or general ability).

Adding a prefix both adds directional meaning and collapses the pair into an ordinary aspectual pair: the unidirectional stem becomes perfective, the multidirectional stem becomes imperfective. E.g. при- (arrival): прийти (perf.) / приходить (imperf.) — to arrive on foot.

items.en: I'm walking to the university right now. (unidirectional)

items.ru: Я хожу в университет каждый день.
items.tr: Ya khozhu v universitet kazhdyy den'.
items.en: I go to the university every day. (multidirectional, habit)
The train arrives at eight o'clock. (prefixed imperf.)
Please close the door. (perf. imperative)

units.summary_ar: صيغ الأمر، والصيغة الشرطية مع «بِي»، والأفعال الانعكاسية بـ«-سيا»، وأفعال الحركة المزدوجة مع البادئات.

units.title: 16. Participles, Verbal Adverbs & Syntax Notes
units.summary: Active/passive and present/past participles, the деепричастие (verbal adverb), plus word order, negation, and questions.

blocks.html: Participles (причастия) are verbal adjectives: they are formed from verbs but decline like adjectives and agree with a noun. Russian has four, crossing voice (active/passive) with tense (present/past). They are largely bookish/written register; speech prefers a который clause.

Present participles form only from imperfective verbs (there is no perfective present). Passive participles form only from transitive verbs. The past passive participle is the most used; its short form builds the periphrastic passive: Книга прочитана (the book has been read), Дом построен (the house has been built).
The verbal adverb / gerund (деепричастие) is an indeclinable form expressing a secondary, simultaneous or prior action by the same subject as the main verb — English '-ing' / 'having ...ed'.

A deeprichastie clause shares the subject of the main clause; a mismatch is a classic error (the Russian equivalent of the 'dangling participle'). Читая книгу, я заснул — 'Reading the book, I fell asleep' (I both read and slept). Reflexive forms keep -сь: улыбаясь (smiling).

Word order is grammatically free but pragmatically governed: Russian tends to place given/topic information first and new/focus information last. The neutral order is Subject–Verb–Object, but reordering shifts emphasis rather than meaning: Книгу читает студент foregrounds 'the book'.

items: Negation: the particle не precedes the negated word: Я не знаю (I don't know). Negating the verb often pulls the object into the genitive: Я не вижу ошибки (I don't see the/an error).

Double (multiple) negation is obligatory: negative pronouns/adverbs co-occur with не: Я ничего не знаю (lit. 'I nothing don't know' = I know nothing); Никто никогда ничего не говорит is perfectly grammatical.
нет ('there is no') governs the genitive: Здесь нет воды (there's no water here); past не было + gen., future не будет + gen.

Yes/no questions are formed by intonation alone (rising pitch on the focus word) — no word order change needed: Ты дома? = 'Are you home?'. The particle ли gives a more formal/indirect yes-no question: Не знаете ли вы…?
Wh-questions use the interrogatives кто, что, где, когда, почему, как, какой, etc., normally clause-initial.

items.en: The student reading the book is my brother. (present active participle)
items.ar: الطالب الذي يقرأ الكتاب هو أخي.

items.en: Having read the letter, she burst into tears. (perfective verbal adverb)
I have never been there. (obligatory double negation)
We don't have time. (нет + genitive)
Do you speak Russian? (yes/no question by intonation)

units.summary_ar: اسما الفاعل والمفعول في صيغتيهما المضارعة والماضية، والظرف الفعلي (دييپريتشاستيه)، بالإضافة إلى ترتيب الكلمات والنفي والأسئلة.
